# Midwest Route Automation Memory

- 2026-07-14 16:15 America/Chicago / 2026-07-14 21:15 UTC: Minnesota three-route implementation pass (`minnesota-route-additions-2`).
  - Added three Minnesota slugs beyond the 112-route Minnesota baseline at run start: `mississippi-river-cass-lake-winnibigoshish`, `st-louis-river-fond-du-lac-oldenburg`, and `cannon-river-riverside-miesville`.
  - Qualification path: current MN DNR state-water-trail pages and maps document Cass Lake / Knutson Dam-to-Lake Winnibigoshish / West Winnie `11.8` miles, Fond du Lac Reservoir-to-Oldenburg Falls `6-7` miles round trip, and Riverside Park-to-Miesville Ravine `6.9` miles; Minnesota public-water-access GIS resolves the route endpoint records with source-backed coordinates and administrator fields.
  - Live gauge verification: MN DNR river-level JSON returned Mississippi River near Bemidji / CSAH 11 site `304` at `110 cfs` on 2026-07-14 14:00, St. Louis River at Scanlon site `239` at `1130 cfs` on 2026-07-14 14:30, and Cannon River at Welch site `13` at `1040 cfs` on 2026-07-14 14:00.
  - Implemented DNR gauge models: Cass Lake-to-Winnie uses upstream same-water-trail Bemidji site `304` with `ideal 63.4-167.6 cfs`, `tooLow 37`, `tooHigh 440.4`; Fond du Lac-to-Oldenburg uses same-corridor Scanlon site `239` with `ideal 1000-3000 cfs`, `tooLow 700`, `tooHigh 5000`; Riverside-to-Miesville uses downstream same-map Welch site `13` with `ideal 300-1540 cfs`, `tooLow 231`, `tooHigh 3000`.
  - Added camping and safety/logistics notes: Cass Lake-to-Winnie has endpoint campground support plus dam/portage, lake-wind, cold-water, and remote-exposure caveats; Fond du Lac-to-Oldenburg is a nearby-basecamp out-and-back with dam, hydro, falls, release, and turnaround caveats; Riverside-to-Miesville is a no-camping day route with light-rapid, snag, fast-rise, private-bank, and Welch-extension access caveats.
  - No route-gallery assets were added. Bounded DNR / Commons / USFS / Recreation.gov / Jay Cooke / Dakota County / same-river image review found no clearly rights-clean exact-route images for local reuse.
  - Reviewed but did not add Snake Silver Star-to-Lower Falls because the DNR gauge timestamp was stale, Red River Kidder-to-Brushvale and Mississippi Fort Ripley-to-Fletcher Creek because endpoint coordinates were not source-backed enough in this run, and Sauk Centre Dam-to-Mill Pond because a current 2026 DNR construction alert affects the exact corridor.

- 2026-07-14 16:09 America/Chicago / 2026-07-14 21:09 UTC: Wisconsin three-route implementation pass (`wisconsin-route-additions`).
  - Added three Wisconsin slugs beyond the 65-route Wisconsin baseline at run start: `wolf-river-pearson-lily`, `black-river-cormican-irving`, and `black-river-irving-melrose`.
  - Qualification path: Wisconsin Trail Guide documents Wolf River W1 Pearson-to-Lily `12.8` miles with WGS84 endpoint coordinates, Class II Wendigo Rapids, and route-specific Langlade-gauge bands; WTG documents Black River BK2 Cormican-to-Irving `12.1` miles and BK3 Irving-to-Melrose `11.8` miles with public landings, WGS84 endpoint coordinates, state-forest/camping context, and Black River Falls gauge bounds of `200` cfs minimum and `3000` cfs maximum.
  - Live gauge verification: USGS Water Services returned Wolf River at Langlade (`04074950`) `438 cfs / 8.09 ft` at 2026-07-14 15:00 CDT, and Black River DS ST Hwy 54 at Black River Falls (`053813595`) `572 cfs / 36.27 ft` at 2026-07-14 15:00 CDT.
  - Implemented gauge models: Wolf W1 uses downstream proxy Langlade with `ideal 500-1100 cfs`, `tooLow 250`, `tooHigh 1800`, and `routeType: 'whitewater'`; Black Cormican-to-Irving uses direct Black River Falls with broad WTG bounds `ideal 200-3000 cfs`, `tooLow 200`, `tooHigh 3000`; Black Irving-to-Melrose uses the same upstream gauge as a proxy with the same WTG bounds and no invented sweet spot.
  - Added camping and safety/logistics notes: Wolf W1 is no-camping and warns on Wendigo portage, boulder gardens, downstream proxy limitations, strainers, and high-water novice risk; Black BK2 has on-route Black River State Forest canoe-campsite support plus Hawk Island riffle, shallow-channel, strainer, and high-water campsite caveats; Black BK3 has sandbar/beach camping context plus private-bank, island-fork, deadfall, and high-water caveats.
  - No route-gallery assets were added. Bounded Rivers.gov / Commons / DNR / Black River State Forest / The Dyrt image review did not produce a clearly rights-clean exact route paddling asset selected for local reuse; a CC0 Commons Black River Falls dam image was not selected because it is dam-infrastructure context rather than exact on-route paddling imagery.

- 2026-06-22 18:35 America/Chicago / 2026-06-22 23:35 UTC: Kentucky three-route implementation pass (`3-new-routes-automation`).

  - Added three internet-sourced Kentucky routes: `south-fork-kentucky-river-bishop-bend-hacker-branch`, `south-fork-kentucky-river-hacker-branch-upper-wolf-creek`, and `levisa-fork-island-creek-cedar-creek`.
  - Qualification path: current KDFWR South Fork page documented the 3.3-mile Bishop Bend-to-Hacker Branch split and the 2.9-mile Hacker Branch-to-Upper Wolf Creek split, with exact access coordinates and official Booneville bands of `350-900 cfs / 4.8-6.0 ft`; same-day direct USGS `03281500` returned `171 cfs / 4.13 ft` at 2026-06-22 18:30 EDT. Current KDFWR Levisa Fork page documented the 1.5-mile Island Creek-to-Cedar Creek split, exact access coordinates, and official Pikeville bands of `800-1400 cfs / 7.4-8.5 ft`; same-day direct USGS `03209500` returned `341 cfs` at 2026-06-22 17:30 EDT and `6.56 ft` at 2026-06-22 18:00 EDT.
  - Added camping and safety/logistics notes for all three routes and reused the existing rights-clean South Fork and Levisa gallery assets for each route.

- 2026-06-22 17:26 America/Chicago / 2026-06-22 22:26 UTC: Kentucky three-route implementation pass (`3-new-routes-automation`).
  - Reused the current route queue after `npm run routes:leads:gather` showed 271 open leads and no `implementation_ready` candidates, then pivoted to internet-first Kentucky sourcing with current KDFWR, KDFWR Blue Water Trail PDFs, and same-day USGS Water Services checks.
  - Added three internet-sourced Kentucky routes: `south-fork-kentucky-river-rocky-branch-cedar-valley`, `south-fork-kentucky-river-cedar-valley-bishop-bend`, and `levisa-fork-jubilee-island-creek`.
  - Qualification path: current KDFWR South Fork page and Blue Water Trail PDF documented the 2.5-mile Rocky Branch-to-Cedar Valley split and the 4.7-mile Cedar Valley-to-Bishop Bend split, with exact access coordinates and official Booneville bands of `350-900 cfs / 4.8-6.0 ft`; same-day direct USGS `03281500` returned `173 cfs / 4.14 ft` at 2026-06-22 17:30 EDT. Current KDFWR Levisa fish page, education page, detailed map PDF, and access-detail pages documented the 3.8-mile Jubilee-to-Island Creek split, exact access coordinates, and official Pikeville bands of `800-1400 cfs / 7.4-8.5 ft`; same-day direct USGS `03209500` returned `341 cfs / 6.56 ft` at 2026-06-22 17:30-18:00 EDT.
  - Added camping and safety/logistics notes for all three routes and wired rights-clean gallery assets for each: two public-domain historical South Fork Kentucky River Commons images and the existing CC0 Levisa Fork Commons image.

  - Kearney Water Trail remains blocked because its direct official gauge is Nebraska DWEE `00145700`, while PaddleTodayV2 still supports only `usgs` and `mn_dnr` live providers.   - The route ships as guarded `minimum-only` with `tooLow: 699`; no app gallery image was added because no rights-clean exact-route local asset was selected.

Platte, Niobrara, and Elkhorn still lack source-backed numeric paddling thresholds tied to selected product-supported gauges; Calamus still lacks current gauge support, a short day reach, and thresholds; Missouri National Recreational River still lacks a selected Nebraska-side public day reach and private-paddlecraft threshold model.
  - Kearney Water Trail remains the closest lead, but its direct official gauge is Nebraska DWEE `00145700`, while PaddleTodayV2 still supports only `usgs` and `mn_dnr` live providers. No app route data, trip details, gallery, or image-audit rows changed.

DCNR supports the exact seven-mile route, Class III-IV hazards, launch-ticket context, PFD/helmet rules, and Bruner Run take-out; AW supports the direct gauge relationship, stage bands, reach features, and endpoint coordinates.
  - Route ships as `routeType: 'whitewater'` with ideal 1.7-4.0 ft, too-low 1.1 ft, and too-high 7.0 ft on the Ohiopyle stage gauge. A Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 Ohiopyle whitewater image is wired into the route gallery at `/gallery/youghiogheny-river-lower-yough-ohiopyle-bruner-run/lower-yough-kayaker.jpg`; the image audit records the approved Commons source and attribution.
  - Lehigh remains `no_live_gauge` because the visible AW gauge is virtual and no direct supported live gauge was selected; Three Rivers remains `threshold_weak` because no endpoint pair plus route-specific numeric model has been selected.

  - Created repo state memory files at `docs/state-route-memories/indiana.md`, `docs/state-route-memories/nebraska.md`, and `docs/state-route-memories/pennsylvania.md`, then added them to the state-memory README.
  - Indiana should start as an existing-candidate blocker-refresh automation: 0 live Indiana routes and 21 existing ledger rows, with Sugar Creek, Blue River, Cedar Creek, Big Pine Creek, Kankakee, Wabash, and White River families as first refresh leads.
  - Nebraska should start as a fresh official-source seed-and-implement automation using Nebraska Game and Parks, NPS, USACE/NRD/city-county sources, and USGS; key quirks are sandy/braided rivers, wind, private land, irrigation/diversion structures, and Niobrara/Missouri River federal context.
  - Pennsylvania should start as a fresh official-source seed-and-implement automation using PFBC, DCNR/ExplorePAtrails, water-trail managers, NPS/USACE/county-city sources, USGS, and AW; key quirks are PFBC/DCNR launch-permit context, large-river segmentation, whitewater protection, and urban water quality.

- 2026-06-04 Missouri implementation pass (`missouri-route-additions`).
  - Re-read the Missouri automation memory path, Missouri state memory, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and image audit context.
  - Reconciled current Missouri coverage before implementation and found no exact or obvious existing V2 slug for `Eleven Point River - Riverton East River Access to Highway 142 River Access`.
  - Added `Eleven Point River - Riverton East River Access to Highway 142 River Access` as `eleven-point-river-riverton-narrows`, bringing current Missouri route coverage to 37 V2 routes in this working tree.
  - Qualification path: Forest Service Riverton East and Highway 142 pages publish open public access, coordinates, concrete ramp/canoe-launch context, parking, toilets, day-use rules, and an explicit Riverton-to-Highway-142 float route; National Rivers Project independently records Riverton East-to-Narrows as an 8.7-mile full-day Forest Service water-trail section; USGS `07071500` exposed recent official legacy values of `503 cfs / 2.64 ft` at `2026-06-03 20:30 CDT`; adjacent Eleven Point V2 routes already use the same direct lower-river Bardley gauge.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` with `tooLow: 300 cfs`, using Rivers.MOHERP Bardley Good-floor context plus adjacent lower-Eleven-Point trip evidence. No ideal range or upper cutoff was inferred.
  - Caveats retained: community threshold source, National Rivers Project spring/early-June season framing, full-day distance, cold water, shoals, snags/root wads, motorized users under the 25 hp limit, rapid rises, Highway 142 as the last Forest Service scenic-river access, and private/scenic-easement land away from legal stops.
  - Reused the approved same-river Commons Eleven Point image already used by adjacent Eleven Point routes and updated the image audit row. Structured ledger now records the candidate as `added`.

  - The route qualified on KDFWR Pool 6 / Hart County evidence: H.H. Wilson Park Ramp to Munfordville-Stovall Park Ramp is a 3.6-mile public-access segment, KDFWR access records provide both ramp coordinates, and KDFWR gives official Green River at Munfordville bands of Low below 300 cfs, Good 300-600 cfs, and High above 600 cfs.
  - Official USGS legacy current conditions for `03308500` showed same-day 635 cfs / 3.41 ft at 2026-06-03 12:00 CDT. Current flow was just above KDFWR's good band, so route copy carries high-water caution rather than ideal-day framing.
  - Caveats added: H.H. Wilson ramp drop, Stovall city-park ramp traffic, broad-river wind, motorboats and anglers, private/muddy banks, storm debris, and upstream release/rain effects. Image audit added no approved image because KDFWR imagery lacks explicit compatible reuse rights and the USGS public-domain Munfordville flood-measurement image was not representative paddling-route imagery.

  - Re-read the Missouri automation memory path, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and Missouri state memory.

  - Added `Big River - Mammoth Access to Merrill Horse Access` as `big-river-mammoth-merrill-horse`, bringing Missouri coverage to 34 V2 routes.
  - Qualification path: MDC confirms Mammoth and Merrill Horse as public Big River accesses and gives the 5.40-mile spacing; official USGS `07018100` exposed same-day values of `1,490 cfs / 5.76 ft` at `2026-06-01 07:00 CDT`; OzarkAnglers ties the Mammoth/Merrill section to the Richwoods gauge and gives route-specific community bands.
  - Implemented a two-sided community threshold model: ordinary float window `100-800 cfs`, `tooLow: 100`, `tooHigh: 1200`; the current review-time value was above the too-high cutoff, so app copy explicitly tells users to skip high/rising water rather than implying the route is currently suitable.
  - Caveats retained: threshold source is community, endpoint coordinates are practical access/gauge-corridor anchors rather than survey-grade ramp points, and users still need same-day checks for wood, gravel riffles, anglers, motorboats, high muddy current, and private banks.
  - No rights-clean gallery image was selected.

  - Implemented as `routeType: whitewater` with mixed `two-sided` cfs scoring: `tooLow: 200`, `idealMin: 3500`, `idealMax: 4000`, `tooHigh: 4000`. Added practical access-area coordinates from USGS/AW for Remmel and Mapcarta/OpenStreetMap for the Whitewater Park.
  - No gallery image was added; Wikimedia Commons has a CC BY-SA Remmel Dam image lead, but no representative exact-route paddling image was ingested in this network-restricted run.

  - Qualification path: MDC confirms Blair Bridge Access as public floating access, Dawt Mill publicly offers floating with upstream shuttle and direct resort-return access, Float Missouri / MDC-reprint mile notes place Blair Bridge and Dawt Mill in the lower North Fork corridor, Rivers.MOHERP has exact Blair-to-Dawt good-condition rows at 428 and 811 cfs, and official USGS `07057500` showed same-day `1,220 cfs / 3.50 ft` at `2026-05-31 15:00 CDT` during review.

  - Qualification path: NPS documents Pruitt-to-Hasty as a 7-mile / 3-5 hour Buffalo National River trip, identifies Hasty as the river-right take-out across from Chimney Rock Bluff, says the Little Buffalo River adds velocity, and says the section is not recommended for beginners. The NPS homepage links the current Float Guide dashboard; indexed dashboard content exposes Pruitt cfs bands. Official USGS `07055680` showed same-day `442 cfs / 4.85 ft` at `2026-05-31 16:30 CDT`, and AW corroborates the exact I-II reach.
  - Threshold model: guarded `minimum-only` at `100 cfs` on direct USGS `07055680`, from the NPS-linked Float Guide's Pruitt Very Low/Low boundary. No ideal range is claimed; NPS's `2,000 cfs` unsafe/flood value remains a caveat.
  - Route-gallery result: no gallery asset added. The NPS route-page image is not clearly reusable for local ingestion, and no rights-clean exact-route Commons/USGS photo was found.
  - Remaining caveats: routeType whitewater, rain dependence, swift water, sharp bends, Little Buffalo velocity increase, strainers/sweepers/fresh flood wood, low-water dragging, high/rising water, cold water, limited cell service, rough rural shuttle roads, and remote rescue exposure.

- 2026-05-31: `Current River - Akers Ferry to Pulltite` (missouri-route-additions).
  - Re-read and reconciled the Missouri automation memory path, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and Missouri-related ledger notes. The route was not already present under an exact or obvious slug; the canonical ledger candidate `mo-current-river-akers-ferry-pulltite` was still `blocked_until_date`.

  - Qualification path: NPS lists Akers to Pulltite as a 9.6-mile / 4-hour Upper Current float, NPS private-vessel guidance supports designated access use, Recreation.gov currently describes Pulltite as year-round with flood-damaged facilities reduced after October 15, 2025 rather than an active closure, USGS `07064533` showed same-day official values of `311 cfs / 1.09 ft` at `2026-05-31 15:30 CDT`, and Rivers.MOHERP provides exact Akers-to-Pulltite good-condition evidence plus nearby Upper Current low-water context.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at `230 cfs` on direct USGS `07064533`. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed; NPS Akers/Pulltite closure stages are included only as high-water cautions.
  - Endpoint/image result: coordinates reuse adjacent implemented Current River access-area anchors for Akers Lower and Pulltite, and the route reuses the approved Commons Current River corridor image already used by adjacent Current River routes.
  - Remaining caveats: Pulltite flood-damage facility limitations and possible repair/closure notices, NPS posted access layout, high or rising water, shallow riffles near the floor, cold spring water, caves, crowding, motorized traffic, limited cell service, and federal river rules.

- 2026-05-31 / 2026-06-01 UTC: `Slate Creek - Lions Club Park to Old Slate Furnace Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `slate-creek-lions-club-old-slate-furnace`, the third Kentucky V2 route.
  - Re-read the Kentucky automation memory path, Kentucky state memory, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and image audit. Current Kentucky coverage before this pass was two routes: `elkhorn-creek-vpa-3-aw-access` and `levisa-fork-cedar-creek-thompson-road`; no duplicate route was added.
  - Qualification path: KDFWR confirms Lions Club Park-to-Old Slate Furnace Park as a 3.7-mile Slate Creek segment, KDFWR access-detail records publish carry-down access and coordinates for both endpoints, KDFWR publishes Low below 100 cfs / Good 100-200 cfs / High above 200 cfs guidance for Howards Mill downstream to the Licking River, and official USGS `03250190` legacy current conditions showed May 31, 2026 instantaneous discharge and gage-height values during this run.
  - Threshold model: official two-sided KDFWR band at 100-200 cfs on the direct Slate Creek USGS gauge. Caveats include the low-head dam at Lions Club Park, launching below the dam, shallow rock bars and dragging below the floor, high-water push above 200 cfs, wood/strainers after rain, and private-bank discipline.
  - Route-gallery result: no gallery asset added. Search found rights-clean Commons/NPS Bourbon Iron Works / Old Slate Furnace imagery and a USGS Slate Creek gauge-work image, but no representative rights-clean paddling-route photo.

- 2026-05-31: Missouri add pass at 5:45 PM America/Chicago (missouri-route-additions).
  - Re-read and reconciled the Missouri route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and Missouri state memory. Current Missouri coverage before this pass was 28 V2 routes; `Big Piney River - Slabtown Recreation Area to Ross Access` was not present under an exact or obvious slug.
  - Added `Big Piney River - Slabtown Recreation Area to Ross Access` as `big-piney-river-slabtown-ross`.
  - Qualification path: Forest Service confirms Slabtown Recreation Area as a Big Piney access with coordinates and describes the Slabtown-to-Ross corridor as narrower, shallower, and riffly; MDC confirms Ross Access as public Big Piney floating/fishing access; Big Piney River Stream Team Watershed Association lists Slabtown-to-Six-Crossings and Six-Crossings-to-Ross mileages; Rivers.MOHERP has an exact Slabtown-to-Ross Bridge row; and official USGS `06930000` showed same-day `395 cfs / 3.21 ft` at `2026-05-31 15:00 CDT`.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at `309 cfs` on direct same-river `USGS 06930000`, reusing the existing Big Piney MoHERP low-water floor. No ideal range or high-water cutoff was claimed.
  - Remaining caveats: this is a long 15.5-to-17-mile day or light overnight, Ross take-out coordinates are OSM-derived but paired with MDC access authority, the route has shallow riffles below Slabtown, private-bank boundaries matter, and high or rising water exceeds the model. Gallery reuses the already approved Commons Big Piney River same-river image.

  - Re-read the Tennessee automation memory path, Tennessee state memory, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and image audit. Current Tennessee coverage before this pass was zero V2 routes; no duplicate route was present.
  - Added `Big South Fork - Burnt Mill Bridge to Leatherwood Ford` as `big-south-fork-burnt-mill-leatherwood`.
  - Qualification path: NPS names Burnt Mill Bridge to Leatherwood Ford as an 11-mile Class III-IV fall/winter/spring run, NPS hosts Leatherwood Ford gauge descriptions, NPS documents access directions plus fast-rise hazards, USGS `03410210` showed same-day official values (`3860 cfs / 9.80 ft` at `2026-05-31 14:30 EDT`), and American Whitewater corroborates the downstream gorge as Class II-IV wilderness whitewater with undercuts, sieves, major rapids, long pools, and limited exits.
  - Threshold model: mixed-source `two-sided` model on direct `USGS 03410210`, with `tooLow 500 cfs`, `idealMin 1000 cfs`, `idealMax 2500 cfs`, and `tooHigh 3500 cfs`. Current review water was above the high cutoff, so the route copy frames that as a high-water caution rather than a recommendation.
  - Remaining caveats: the route is marked `routeType: 'whitewater'`; this is advanced gorge water with undercut sandstone, sieves, hydraulics, pin rocks, strainers, cold swims, fast rain rises, limited exits, steep walk-outs, remote rescue exposure, and park access/road checks. No route-gallery image was added because the qualifying Commons Leatherwood Ford image could not be fetched in the sandbox.

  - Qualification path: MDC confirms Delaware Town and Shelvin Rock as public James River day-use accesses with boat ramps and parking; James River Basin Partnership names Delaware Town-to-Shelvin Rock as a float-series route; Rivers.MOHERP lists exact Delaware-to-Shelvin rows at 6.5 miles on the Boaz gauge, including 275 cfs as about the minimum recommended water level; and official USGS `07052250` legacy current conditions exposed same-day May 30, 2026 discharge/stage values during this run.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 275 cfs on USGS `07052250`. No ideal range or upper cutoff was inferred because the threshold evidence is community-calibrated rather than an official manager-published paddling band.
  - Caveats: Delaware Town coordinate is from a public paddling directory paired with MDC access confirmation, threshold support is community-level, ledge-rock drops and shallow summer riffles need judgment, high/rising water has no defended cutoff, private banks are not assumed public, and no rights-clean gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed; `npm.cmd run build` passed; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed in the existing Vitest/esbuild sandbox config-loading path with access denied reading `../../..` and could not resolve `vitest.config.ts`.

  - Added `Big Piney River - Boiling Spring Access to Mason Bridge Access` as `big-piney-river-boiling-spring-mason-bridge`.
  - Qualification path: MDC describes Boiling Spring-to-Mason Bridge as a six-mile Big Piney daylong float, Category I / Easy except during high water; MDC confirms Boiling Spring Access as public Big Piney access; MDC Mason Bridge map material confirms parking and boat-ramp features; Big Piney River STWA lists Boiling Spring-to-Mason Bridge as river mile 26.6 to 32.5; official USGS `06930000` legacy current conditions showed same-day May 30, 2026 discharge/stage; and adjacent V2 Big Piney routes already use the same direct gauge.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 309 cfs on USGS `06930000`, reusing the existing Big Piney MoHERP low-water floor. No ideal range or upper cutoff was inferred from community bands.
  - Caveats: Mason Bridge take-out coordinate is Mapcarta/OpenStreetMap-derived rather than MDC-published, Boiling Spring remains the existing topo-derived spring/access anchor paired with MDC access confirmation, threshold support is community-level, high/rising water can exceed the easy rating, private banks require legal stops only, and no rights-clean gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: ledger JSON parse passed; `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed with route audit for 204 routes; `npm.cmd run build` passed and generated the new route page; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully but Vitest failed to load config because esbuild hit sandbox access-denied while reading `../../..`.

  - Qualification path: Friends of the Kaw documents Edwardsville at river mile 16.6 with GPS `39.05056, -94.81665`, Turner Bridge at river mile 9.2 with GPS `39.09396, -94.71176`, Turner Bridge as the next day-trip access downstream, and the WaterOne low-head dam at river mile 14.8 with a required river-left-only portage. The WaterOne page warns not to run the dam or river-right notch and describes a rough 100+ yard loose-rock carry; River Access Tips and Paddle FAQ support hazard checks, Kansas public-river/ramp context, and 5,000/8,000 cfs safety bands. USACE Kansas River recreation material supports the low-side and broad optimal-impact bands. USGS `06892350` Kansas River at De Soto is used as the conservative upstream lower-Kaw discharge proxy already used by adjacent lower-Kaw routes.
  - Threshold model: guarded two-sided on the De Soto proxy gauge with ideal 1,500-5,000 cfs, too-low 1,000 cfs, and too-high 8,000 cfs. Source strength remains `mixed`, not route-specific official, so route copy states the proxy-gauge and broad Kaw calibration limits clearly.
  - Caveats included: mandatory WaterOne portage, no running the dam, no river-right notch, rough loose-rock carry, high-water slick-mud landing risk, same-day visual checks, private banks, few sandbars, wind, high-water sandbar loss, low-water slow travel, and Turner Bridge rough/muddy parking. No route-gallery image was selected.

Reconciled current Kansas inventory first and found twelve existing Kansas routes before this add: `kansas-river-lawrence-8th-eudora`, `kansas-river-eudora-de-soto`, `kansas-river-de-soto-edwardsville`, `kansas-river-junction-city-ogden`, `kansas-river-ogden-manhattan`, `kansas-river-manhattan-st-george`, `kansas-river-st-george-wamego`, `kansas-river-wamego-belvue`, `kansas-river-belvue-kaw-river-state-park`, `kansas-river-topeka-water-plant-seward`, `kansas-river-lecompton-lawrence-riverfront`, and `kansas-river-turner-bridge-kaw-point`; no duplicate was added.
  - Added `Kansas River - Seward Avenue / Fool's Landing Access Ramp to Lecompton / Rising Sun Access Ramp` as `kansas-river-seward-lecompton`.
  - Qualification path: Friends of the Kaw documents Seward/Fool's Landing at river mile 77 with GPS `39.06036, -95.59468`, Lecompton/Rising Sun at river mile 64.3 with GPS `39.05057, -95.38764`, the 12.7-mile downstream spacing, both public ramp contexts, and the Tecumseh low-head-dam hazard one mile below Seward with no public bank portage or road access. Friends of the Kaw safety material supplies the 5,000 cfs novice and 8,000 cfs all-paddler high-side bands. USACE Kansas River recreation material supports the low-side and broad optimal-impact bands. USGS `06888990` Kansas River above Topeka Weir is used as an upstream Topeka-corridor proxy already supported by adjacent Topeka-area routes.
  - Reconciled the existing Lecompton-to-Lawrence shared endpoint to the current Friends of the Kaw Lecompton coordinate `39.05057, -95.38764` and current access details.
  - Threshold model: guarded two-sided on the Topeka Weir proxy gauge with ideal 1,500-5,000 cfs, too-low 1,000 cfs, and too-high 8,000 cfs. Source strength remains `mixed`, not route-specific official, so route copy states the proxy-gauge and broad Kaw calibration limits clearly.
  - Caveats included: experienced-paddler-only Tecumseh passage, no public dam portage, proxy-gauge limits, same-day visual checks, private banks, wind, high-water sandbar loss, low-water slow travel, and ramp gate/silt/mud conditions. No route-gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: ledger JSON parse passed; `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed with route-data audit for 199 routes; `npm.cmd run build` passed and generated `/rivers/kansas-river-seward-lecompton/`; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed in the existing Vite/Vitest/esbuild sandbox config-loading path (`../../..` access denied and unable to resolve `vitest.config.ts`).

- 2026-05-30: Missouri implementation pass at 7:31 PM America/Chicago (2026-05-31T00:31Z).
  - Re-read and reconciled live Missouri inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and the Missouri automation memory path. Current Missouri coverage before this add was `current-river-cedar-grove-akers`, `jacks-fork-river-buck-hollow-rymers`, `jacks-fork-river-alley-spring-chilton`, `current-river-waymeyer-van-buren`, `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`, `meramec-river-onondaga-campbell-bridge`, `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park`, `bryant-creek-sycamore-warren-bridge`, `big-piney-river-east-gate-bookers-bend`, `big-piney-river-sandy-shoals-boiling-spring`, `james-river-shelvin-rock-hooten-town`, and `james-river-hl-kerr-ralph-cox`; no duplicate route was added.
  - Added `Eleven Point River - Turner Mill South River Access to Whitten River Access` as `eleven-point-river-turner-mill-south-whitten`.
  - Qualification path: Forest Service pages list Turner Mill South and Whitten as open public accesses, publish coordinates, and identify Whitten as the next access downriver from Turner Mill South. MDC confirms public access at Turner Mill South and Whitten and describes the cold spring-fed route character below Greer Spring. The route uses direct same-river `USGS 07071500` at Bardley, already product-used by `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 300 cfs from Rivers.MOHERP Bardley gauge support. MoHERP rates Bardley Good starting at 300 cfs and includes exact Turner Mill-to-Whitten evidence marked Good at 797 cfs. No ideal range or upper cutoff was inferred because the threshold support is community-calibrated rather than manager-published.
  - Caveats included: Forest Service river-mile notes imply about 6.1 miles while MoHERP rounds the trip to 8.0 miles, rural access roads, cold water, shoals, rapid rises after rain, wood, motorized users under the 25 hp allowance, and private/scenic-easement land away from public accesses or legal stops. No route-gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: ledger JSON parse passed; `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed with route-data audit for 198 routes; `npm.cmd run build` passed and generated `/rivers/eleven-point-river-turner-mill-south-whitten/`; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed in the existing Vite/Vitest/esbuild sandbox config-loading path (`../../..` access denied and unable to resolve `vitest.config.ts`).

Current Missouri coverage before this pass was `current-river-cedar-grove-akers`, `jacks-fork-river-buck-hollow-rymers`, `jacks-fork-river-alley-spring-chilton`, `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`, `meramec-river-onondaga-campbell-bridge`, `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park`, `bryant-creek-sycamore-warren-bridge`, `big-piney-river-east-gate-bookers-bend`, `big-piney-river-sandy-shoals-boiling-spring`, `james-river-shelvin-rock-hooten-town`, and `james-river-hl-kerr-ralph-cox`; no duplicate route was added.
  - Added `Current River - Waymeyer Access to Van Buren Riverfront Park` as `current-river-waymeyer-van-buren`.
  - Qualification path: NPS lists Waymeyer to Van Buren as a 3-hour Lower Current section and names Waymeyer as a popular lower Current put-in; NPS access update says Waymeyer continues as a non-commercial floater access while warning about erosion and crowding limits; MDC confirms Van Buren Riverfront Park, boat use, and an area map with boat ramp; USGS `07067000` Current River at Van Buren showed same-day 2026-05-30 discharge and gage-height observations; Rivers.MOHERP ties the Van Buren gauge to ratings and exact Waymeyer-to-Van-Buren trip evidence.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 700 cfs on USGS `07067000`. Source strength is `community`; no ideal range or high-water cutoff was claimed. NPS Van Buren Bridge 5.00 ft closure level is used only as a high-water caution.
  - Caveats included: Waymeyer unloading/crowding limits, lower-river motorboats and summer crowds, slick/crowded ramps, private banks near Van Buren, high/rising water, and same-day NPS closure/access checks. No route-gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: ledger JSON parse passed, `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed with route-data audit for 193 routes, `npm.cmd run build` passed and generated the new route page, and `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed in the existing Vite/Vitest/esbuild sandbox config-loading path (`../../..` access denied and unable to resolve `vitest.config.ts`).

- 2026-05-30: Kansas implementation pass at 5:10 PM America/Chicago (2026-05-30T22:10Z).
  - Re-read the Kansas automation memories, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and image audit. Reconciled current Kansas inventory first and found seven existing Kansas routes, `kansas-river-lawrence-8th-eudora`, `kansas-river-eudora-de-soto`, `kansas-river-de-soto-edwardsville`, `kansas-river-st-george-wamego`, `kansas-river-manhattan-st-george`, `kansas-river-topeka-water-plant-seward`, and `kansas-river-turner-bridge-kaw-point`; no duplicate was added.
  - Added `Kansas River - Wamego Access Ramp to Belvue Access Ramp` as `kansas-river-wamego-belvue`.
  - Qualification path: Friends of the Kaw documents Wamego at river mile 128.5 with GPS `39.19828, -96.30537`, Belvue at river mile 119 with GPS `39.21644, -96.17678`, the 9.5-mile downstream access sequence, and the Belvue partial low-head dam at river mile 120 one mile above the take-out. Friends of the Kaw safety material supplies the 5,000 cfs novice and 8,000 cfs all-paddler high-side bands. USACE Kansas River recreation material supports the low-side and broad optimal-impact bands. USGS `06887500` Kansas River at Wamego is used as the direct put-in-corridor gauge.
  - Threshold model: guarded two-sided on the Wamego gauge with ideal 1,500-5,000 cfs, too-low 1,000 cfs, and too-high 8,000 cfs. Source strength remains `mixed`, not route-specific official, so route copy states the broad Kaw calibration clearly.
  - Caveats included: this is not a novice float, stay river left past the Belvue partial low-head dam, avoid right-side dam structures, watch exposed or submerged rocks, scout Belvue before launching, and check same-day ramp, wind, sandbar, private-bank, and high-water conditions. No route-gallery image was selected.
  - Validation: ledger JSON parse passed, `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed with route-data audit for 192 routes, `npm.cmd run build` passed and generated the new route page, and `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed in the existing Vite/Vitest/esbuild sandbox config-loading path (`../../..` access denied and unable to resolve `vitest.config.ts`).

- 2026-05-30: Kansas implementation pass at 4:11 PM America/Chicago (2026-05-30T21:11Z).
  - Re-read the Kansas automation memories, live route inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and image audit. Reconciled current Kansas inventory first and found five existing Kansas routes, `kansas-river-lawrence-8th-eudora`, `kansas-river-de-soto-edwardsville`, `kansas-river-st-george-wamego`, `kansas-river-manhattan-st-george`, and `kansas-river-topeka-water-plant-seward`; no duplicate was added.
  - Added `Kansas River - Turner Bridge Access Ramp to Kaw Point Park Access Ramp` as `kansas-river-turner-bridge-kaw-point`.
  - Qualification path: Friends of the Kaw documents the Turner Bridge public ramp at river mile 9.2 with GPS `39.09396, -94.71176`, the Kaw Point Park public ramp at river mile 0 with GPS `39.11601, -94.61203`, the 9.2-mile downstream access sequence, controlled lower-river banks with almost no sandbars, Kaw Point park-hour/event caveats, and the Missouri River confluence 200 yards downstream. Friends of the Kaw safety material supplies the 5,000 cfs novice and 8,000 cfs all-paddler high-side bands. USACE Kansas River recreation material supports the low-flow and no-impact calibration. USGS `06892350` Kansas River at De Soto is used as a conservative upstream lower-Kaw discharge proxy because the closer Kansas City gauge `06892950` is stage-only and does not compute discharge.
  - Threshold model: guarded two-sided on the De Soto proxy gauge with ideal 1,500-5,000 cfs, too-low 1,000 cfs, and too-high 8,000 cfs. Source strength remains `mixed`, not route-specific official, so route copy states the broad Kaw calibration and proxy limitation clearly.
  - Caveats included: finish at Kaw Point rather than continuing onto the Missouri River, controlled lower banks with almost no sandbars, Kaw Point 10 pm to 7 am park closure and special-event access caveats, Turner Bridge mud/rough parking, private banks, wind, storms, bridge current, floating wood, high water, and same-day ramp checks.
  - No route-gallery image was selected. - 2026-05-30: Created next five state route-addition automations for Minnesota, Ohio, Indiana, Nebraska, and Kansas.
  - Selection rationale: continue the existing Midwest-first expansion path, then move outward into adjacent Plains states. Minnesota and Ohio already have route inventory or queues but lacked focused state automations; Indiana is the next central Midwest gap; Nebraska and Kansas continue the outward Plains expansion after South Dakota, Missouri, Iowa, and Utah.
  - Created repo memory files under `automations/<state>-route-additions/memory.md` and matching Codex memory files under `C:\Users\Yerff\.codex\automations\<state>-route-additions\memory.md`.
  - Created paused Codex app cron automations: `minnesota-route-additions-2`, `ohio-route-additions-2`, `indiana-route-additions-2`, `nebraska-route-additions-2`, and `kansas-route-additions-2`.
  - State-specific guardrails added: Minnesota should prefer DNR interpreted state-water-trail gauges and avoid saturated-corridor duplication; Ohio should lean on ODNR water-trail/scenic-river maps and treat lowhead dams and weak numeric thresholds as primary gates; Indiana should verify navigability/OHWM, public endpoint authority, and low-head-dam restrictions before shipping; Nebraska should treat private streambeds, fences/obstructions, braided shallow channels, wind, and sandbars as first-class caveats; Kansas should focus first on the public Kansas, Arkansas, and Missouri Rivers because most other streams require permission/private-access clarity.
  - No app route was added by this setup pass. Only automation memory/config files were intentionally changed.

  - Marked the route `routeType: 'whitewater'` to protect casual Explore discovery because AW is the threshold source and the reach is cold swift dam-release tailwater.   - Re-read and reconciled live V2 inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, image audit, and the Illinois automation memory path. Existing Illinois coverage checked first: `Vermilion River - Lowell to Oglesby / Wildcat Canyon` remains live as `vermilion-river-lowell-oglesby`; no duplicate was added.

Fox River is open from Stratton Lock and Dam south to Bridge Street Bridge in Millington effective 2026-05-07; the Upper Fox / Chain O'Lakes remains reopened under no-wake restriction effective 2026-05-04; Middle Fork Vermilion is open effective 2026-04-06 from the Illinois state line to Potomac; and Kishwaukee is open effective 2026-04-24 on the lower closure reach.
  - Added `Kishwaukee River - Hickory Bills Canoe Launch to Distillery Road Conservation Area` as `kishwaukee-river-hickory-bills-distillery`. Qualification path: BCCD/Openlands water-trail materials identify Hickory Bills Island and Distillery Road as established public launches; BCCD confirms Distillery Road has a canoe launch, parking, restroom, picnic tables, and public Kishwaukee access; BCCD directs paddlers at Distillery-area sites to USGS `05438500` at Belvidere for real-time flow/stage; the USGS legacy current page showed recent official Belvidere discharge/stage through 2026-05-29; and Wisconsin River Trips gives same-gauge numeric Belvidere-corridor flow bands.
  - Threshold model: conservative two-sided community-source model on USGS `05438500`: `tooLow` 200 cfs, useful window 201-600 cfs, and `tooHigh` 600 cfs. This follows Wisconsin River Trips' rough estimates and intentionally avoids overclaiming precision above the source. Route copy preserves Illinois caveats: use only established public launches, do not assume private-bank landing rights, check BCCD hunting/winter closures and IDNR notices, and avoid high/dirty post-rain water, strainers, wastewater context, and shallow riffles.
  - Photo status: no route-gallery image was added. A rights-clean Commons Kishwaukee image exists from DeKalb, but it is not route-specific enough for this Belvidere-to-Distillery route; the image audit was updated with a no-image reason.
  - Validation after app/data/doc changes: `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed, ledger JSON parse passed, `npm.cmd run build` passed, and `git diff --check` passed with existing line-ending warnings. `npm.cmd test` failed after its own typecheck phase because Vitest/esbuild could not read `../../..` and could not resolve `vitest.config.ts` in this sandbox.

- 2026-05-26: Missouri implementation pass at 4:40 PM America/Chicago.
  - Re-read and reconciled the live V2 inventory, trip details, Midwest memory, structured ledger, route-addition requirements, and automation memory. Existing Missouri routes remained `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`, `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park`, `bryant-creek-sycamore-warren-bridge`, and `james-river-shelvin-rock-hooten-town`; none were duplicated.
  - Added `Jacks Fork River - Buck Hollow / Highway 17 Bridge to Rymers` as `jacks-fork-river-buck-hollow-rymers`.
  - Qualification path: NPS names Highway 17 / Buck Hollow to Rymers as a standard Jacks Fork float and lists both as park river access points; the NPS brochure gives access directions and river-mile context; USGS `07065200` Mountain View showed same-day May 26 discharge and gage-height observations; Rivers.MOHERP has Mountain View gauge ratings plus Buck Hollow-to-Rymers trip rows; Southwest Paddler and Paddling.com supplied endpoint coordinates; MDC supports the upper-Jacks seasonal/remoteness framing.
  - Threshold model: `minimum-only` at 100 cfs on USGS `07065200`. This is intentionally conservative and does not claim an ideal range or upper cutoff because the numeric guidance is community-calibrated rather than official manager-published bands.
  - Remaining caveats: upper Jacks Fork is seasonal and remote, current review value was below the floor, low water can require dragging/poling/lining, storms can raise the narrow valley quickly, route has Class I-II moving-water character and is marked `routeType: 'whitewater'`, coordinates are from paddling-location guides rather than an NPS coordinate table, and no rights-clean exact-route image was selected.
  - Validation: `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed. `npm.cmd test` reran typecheck successfully, then failed when Vitest/esbuild could not read `../../..` and could not resolve `vitest.config.ts` from the sandbox. `npm.cmd run build` reran typecheck successfully, then failed on the same sandbox read restriction plus dependency-resolution errors for `aria-query` / `axobject-query`. `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only. Ledger JSON parsed successfully after stripping the existing UTF-8 BOM.

    - `Missouri National Recreational River day-trip segments` -> remains `research_later`.     - `Jacks Fork River - Bay Creek to Alley Spring` -> `no_live_gauge`.
    - NPS supports Bay Creek to Alley as a standard 4-hour Jacks Fork section, MoHERP has Mountain View gauge ratings and route-family trip evidence, and local outfitters describe Bay Creek-to-Alley as a normal 7-mile trip when depth permits. The route was not added because USGS `07065200` showed latest visible discharge and gage-height values from May 25, 2026 during this May 26 run, not same-day current data.
  - Reviewed fresh lower Meramec / Eureka gauge cluster:
    - `Meramec River - Pacific Palisades Access to Allenton Access` -> `threshold_weak`.
    - MDC gives an unusually clean public route shape by saying Allenton is 6.90 river miles downriver from Pacific Palisades, but MoHERP's Eureka gauge page currently has no evaluative discharge thresholds or trip rows, and USGS `07019000` latest visible values were May 25 rather than same-day May 26 in the browser view.
  - Net result: 2 Missouri candidates reviewed, 0 promoted to `likely_addable`, 0 promoted to `needs_manual_coordinates`, 0 routes added, no app code changes, and no npm validation run.

## Added

- 2026-05-26: `James River - Shelvin Rock Access to Hooten Town Access`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `james-river-shelvin-rock-hooten-town`.
  - Reconciliation result: existing Missouri routes `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`, `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park`, and `bryant-creek-sycamore-warren-bridge` were already live with matching `added` ledger records, so none were duplicated.
  - Qualification path: MDC confirms Shelvin Rock and Hooten Town as public James River day-use accesses with boat ramps and parking; Rivers.MOHERP names exact Shelvin Rock-to-Hootentown 6.2-mile trip rows tied to `USGS 07052500`; the May 26 12:35 PM recheck found current Galena values on MoHERP after earlier stale-data blockers; and USGS `07052500` (`James River at Galena, MO`) is a standard product-ready live gauge.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 200 cfs on the Galena gauge. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the level support is community trip evidence rather than an official manager-published paddling band.
  - Remaining caveats: endpoint coordinates remain practical public-location anchors paired with MDC access confirmation, both MDC endpoints are day-use/no-camping areas, low water can mean dragging and slow pools, high/rising water and fresh wood deserve extra caution, summer traffic can be heavy, and private banks away from public/legal stops should be respected.
  - Route-gallery result: no rights-clean exact-route asset was selected.

- 2026-05-26: `Skunk Creek - Legacy Park to Farm Field Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `skunk-creek-legacy-park-farm-field`.
  - Reconciliation result: `Big Sioux River - Farm Field Park to Rotary Park` and `Split Rock Creek - Split Rock Park to Palisades State Park` were already live with matching `added` ledger records, so neither was duplicated. The prior Skunk Creek candidate had been parked at `no_live_gauge`, but this run found same-day USGS values and promoted it instead of creating a new candidate.
  - Qualification path: City of Sioux Falls confirms Legacy Park and Farm Field Park as public park accesses with boat/canoe/kayak access and coordinates; Sioux Empire Paddlers names the Legacy Park-to-Farm Field segment as 5 miles and gives a 4.5 ft good-paddling floor tied to the Skunk Creek Sioux Falls flow link; USGS `06481500` (`Skunk Creek at Sioux Falls, SD`) showed same-day May 26, 2026 values during review: 35.8 cfs and 2.96 ft at 11:30 CDT.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 4.5 ft. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the level support is local-community guidance rather than an official manager-published paddling band.
  - Remaining caveats: the current May 26 reading was below the app floor, so the route may be too shallow today; urban Skunk Creek water quality, E. coli monitoring May through September, worse water after rain, shallow scraping below 4.5 ft, storm debris, bridge approaches, Big Sioux continuation hazards below Farm Field, and possible fences or unexpected obstructions on South Dakota navigable streams.
  - Route-gallery result: no rights-clean exact-route asset was selected.

- 2026-05-26: `Big Sioux River - Farm Field Park to Rotary Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `big-sioux-river-farm-field-rotary`.
  - Reconciliation result: `Split Rock Creek - Split Rock Park to Palisades State Park` was already live as `split-rock-creek-split-rock-park-palisades` with a matching ledger record, so it was not duplicated. The broad `Big Sioux River - Jay Heath Canoe and Kayak Trail` corridor remained too broad for app segmentation, but this shorter in-town Farm Field-to-Rotary segment cleared the bar.
  - Qualification path: City of Sioux Falls confirms Farm Field Park and Rotary Park as public kayak/canoe accesses with coordinates and posted park hours; Sioux Empire Paddlers names Farm Field-to-Rotary as a 4.15-mile Big Sioux in-town segment and gives a 250 cfs good-paddling floor tied to the Big Sioux River Sioux Falls flow link; USGS `06482000` (`Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls, SD`) is a direct product-ready gauge at the put-in corridor.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 250 cfs. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the level support is local-community guidance rather than an official manager-published paddling band.
  - Remaining caveats: urban Big Sioux water quality, E. coli monitoring May through September, worse water after rain, shallow scraping below the 250 cfs floor, storm debris, bridge approaches, downstream downtown/falls/low-head-dam hazards if users continue past Rotary, and possible fences or unexpected obstructions on South Dakota navigable streams.
  - Route-gallery result: no rights-clean exact-route asset was selected.

- 2026-04-27: `Otter Tail River - Wannigan Road to Riverside Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as a conservative proxy-gauge route with official MN DNR route support, official public-water-access records for both endpoints, and official MN DNR interpretation bands on downstream `MN DNR 344` (`Otter Tail River nr Battle Lake, CR72`).
  - Recheck that changed the outcome: the route was previously parked under `no_live_gauge` when DNR-only live feeds were treated as blocked. The current app supports MN DNR gauges, so the remaining question became gauge fit rather than gauge availability.
  - Remaining caveat: threshold confidence is intentionally lower than a direct-gauge route because the Battle Lake gauge sits downstream of the Frazee-area day trip. The app uses a minimum-only floor and keeps route notes explicit about late-spring water, shallow main-channel picking, rock weirs, faster water near Riverside Park, and the low utility pipe past the take-out.

- 2026-04-27: `Big Fork River - Highway 6 South to Highway 6 North`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 with official MN DNR river-level bands on direct `MN DNR 281`.
  - Recheck that changed the outcome: the route was previously parked under `no_live_gauge` when DNR-only live feeds were treated as blocked, but the current app supports MN DNR gauges. MN DNR explicitly recommends this 15.7-mile route, public-water-access data resolves both Highway 6 endpoints with access IDs `WAS01794` and `WAS01792`, and the Craigsville/Highway 6 gauge sits at the put-in river mile.
  - Remaining caveat: this is a remote long-day or overnight route; DNR's general Big Fork low-water warning also references the upstream Highway 38 / Bigfork gauge, so the route notes preserve that supplemental caution while scoring from the closer Craigsville gauge.

- 2026-04-26: `North Fork Crow River - Rockford Access to Riverside County Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 with official MN DNR river-level bands on direct `MN DNR 310`.
  - Supplemental research added: MN DNR's Rockford Access guide names this exact 8.5-mile day trip, the Minnesota public-water-access layer confirms both endpoints with access IDs `WAS03086` and `WAS01955`, Wright County confirms Riverside County Park public river access and campsite facilities, and the DNR Riverside Access guide adds the practical note that the take-out is somewhat steep but launchable for small boats/canoes.
  - Remaining caveat: this uses the same lower North Fork Crow gauge family as the existing Riverside-to-Dayton route; it is useful as a shorter day-trip option, but same-day wood and shallow-gravel checks still matter.

  - Remaining caveat: this exact 6.4-mile segment is map/access-supported rather than highlighted by DNR as a recommended day trip, so notes keep it framed as a compact local level-first route.

## Likely addable

- None yet beyond what is already in the implementation queue.
- 2026-04-27 07:28 America/Chicago: bounded fresh Minnesota-first sweep produced no new implementation-ready route. Minnesota River Map 5 (Mankato-to-Seven-Mile / Le Sueur-to-Henderson) still reads as broad easy-stretch guidance rather than a cleaner exact recommended day trip than the already-live Mankato and Henderson routes, and the lower Blue Earth / Watonwan confluence corridor still does not beat the existing Rapidan add plus the parked Watonwan research-later hold.
- 2026-04-27 07:40 America/Chicago: conservative no-add pass. Re-read the live V2 inventory, route details, triage memory, and candidate ledger exactly as instructed, then stopped without reopening the same saturated Minnesota River Map 5 and lower Blue Earth / Watonwan clusters from 07:28 because no new evidence path had emerged in the meantime. Per the run rules, there were still no queued `likely_addable` candidates and no fresh new-evidence candidate strong enough to outrank the preserved `needs_manual_coordinates` queue, so this remained a short bounded no-add pass rather than a recycled shortlist review. No candidate statuses changed, no code changed, and `npm test` / `npm run build` were not run because no product files changed.
- 2026-04-27 07:48 America/Chicago: fresh Mississippi headwaters gauge-cluster pass still produced no new implementation-ready route. Segment 1 (`Lake Itasca to Coffee Pot Landing`) and segment 2 (`Cass Lake to Lake Winnibigoshish`) are both real official recommended trips, but this evidence path is now lower-yield for PaddleTodayV2 because the blocker is not route legitimacy; it is live-gauge operability plus lake/open-water complexity. `Lake Itasca to Coffee Pot Landing` was preserved as `no_live_gauge` because the current official page gives only qualitative riffle / vegetation / wave hazard context without a route-operable live gauge model. `Cass Lake to Lake Winnibigoshish` was also preserved as `no_live_gauge` because the official route depends on big-lake transitions and the page explicitly warns that paddling across Cass and Winnibigoshish lakes is not recommended. Fresh-discovery takeaway: future Mississippi work should keep favoring river-only direct-gauge corridors over remaining headwaters/lake-transition recommendation blocks unless a stronger official gauge path appears.
- 2026-04-27 08:08 America/Chicago: bounded Minnesota-first gauge-cluster pass produced no new implementation-ready route, but it did surface one fresh hold worth keeping. `Zumbro River - Zumbro Falls to Hammond` was parked at `threshold_weak` because MN DNR explicitly recommends the exact 7-mile main-stem route, the direct `USGS 05374000` Zumbro Falls gauge is a clean live story, and the map text names `Village Park` at Hammond as the take-out; the blocker is that the best numeric support surfaced on this pass is still neighboring-reach mixed evidence from the longer `Miles Paddled Zumbro River II` run using the same put-in/gauge rather than a clean Hammond-specific ladder. Also logged two cluster outcomes so future runs rotate away faster: the Crow Wing Map 2 / Nimrod family is saturated because `Little White Dog to Cottingham County Park` is already live and the farther-downstream same-gauge subreaches still do not have a defensible threshold carryover, and the Vermilion `Twomile Creek to Eightmile Creek` corridor was not reopened because it is already parked at `research_later` and this pass did not surface any genuinely new official gauge or threshold source.

Reusing the Northfield gauge for the full Dundas-to-Hannah's-Bend reach would be guesswork across a dam-controlled lake section.

The route still looks like a map-derived urban subsegment rather than an officially recommended day trip, and I did not find route-level threshold support strong enough to separate this reach from the broader St. Paul pool conditions without guesswork.

- 2026-04-24: `Mississippi River - North Mississippi Park to East River Flats Park`
  - This fills the obvious geographic gap between the rejected `Coon Rapids Dam to North Mississippi Park` reach and the already-added `East River Flats to Hidden Falls` trip.
  - Blocker: I did not find an official recommended-trip or route-specific threshold package for this exact North-Mississippi-to-East-River-Flats corridor. The route definition would still be a stitched metro segment, and the available official gauge ladders do not clearly resolve that corridor as a trustworthy standalone PaddleToday route.
  - Retry only if: MN DNR, NPS, or strong same-route evidence clearly names this exact reach as a standalone trip and supports a defensible live gauge pairing.

- 2026-04-24: `Maquoketa River - Mon-Maq Dam to Pictured Rocks Park`
  - The corridor still looks directionally useful because the Monticello / North Fork Maquoketa access family appears real and the downstream park finish is product-shaped.

- 2026-04-26: `Zumbro River - Village Park to Theilman Access`
  - Fresh same-gauge follow-on preserved from the Zumbro Falls cluster after rechecking the official map PDF instead of the vaguer route page alone.
  - Official support improved on this pass: MN DNR Map 2 clearly names `Village Park` at Hammond and `Theilman` carry-in access on the same main stem.
  - Live-gauge support is directionally strong: `USGS 05374000` at Zumbro Falls is on the same main stem and already underpins nearby shipped Zumbro routes.
  - Blocker: threshold support is still a stitched mixed-source story from neighboring Miles Paddled reaches (`Zumbro River II` and `Zumbro River III`) rather than a clean exact-route ladder for Hammond-to-Theilman itself.
  - Retry only if: MN DNR, USGS-adjacent guidance, or strong same-route evidence ties the Zumbro Falls gauge directly to the Hammond-to-Theilman corridor with a defensible floor or range.
  - Blocker: I did not find a trustworthy enough current package of official endpoint authority and route-level threshold support to clear the V2 bar. The Iowa DNR water-trail PDF path was not cleanly recoverable during this pass, Jones County MyCountyParks endpoint pages for `Upper Mon-Maq Access` / `Pictured Rocks Access` returned generic error pages, and I did not find defensible numeric paddling guidance tying a live gauge to this exact route.
  - Retry only if: Iowa DNR or Jones County publishes reachable endpoint pages or map materials for both accesses and a defensible numeric live-gauge model appears for this exact Mon-Maq-to-Pictured-Rocks corridor.

- 2026-04-24: `Namekagon River - County Road K Landing to Riverside Landing`
  - NPS support is strong for the broader Riverway corridor: NPS maps clearly split the lower Namekagon into `Map 3 - Trego to Riverside Landing`, and the current-conditions dataset still publishes the Leonards gauge (`150-230 cfs` normal, `>650 cfs` high) for the Namekagon.
  - Blocker: I still did not find route-specific threshold support for this exact County Road K to Riverside subreach. NPS Map 3 does describe the downstream County Road K corridor qualitatively (narrow riffles, several Class I rapids before the confluence, more rapids approaching Riverside, low-water challenge on the St. Croix side), but that is not the same as a defensible numeric model for this exact segment. Shipping it would mean porting the broader Leonards guidance onto a more technical lower subreach without enough proof.
  - Retry only if: NPS or strong same-route evidence ties the County Road K to Riverside subreach itself to a defensible numeric live-gauge model rather than only to the broader lower-Namekagon current-conditions panel.

  - Route-candidate ledger was normalized from placeholder form into a real queue so implementation runs can prefer genuinely pre-vetted routes once any likely-addable candidates appear.

- 2026-04-27: `Mississippi River - Steamboat Access to Blackberry Bridge Access` (`threshold_weak`)
  - Fresh upper-Mississippi discovery from a new direct-USGS gauge family.
  - Official support is real: MN DNR explicitly recommends this exact 8-mile day trip and clearly names `Steamboat Access` and `Blackberry Bridge Access` as the endpoints.
  - Live-gauge support is directionally strong: `USGS 05211000` (`Mississippi River at Grand Rapids, MN`) is the cleanest live source on the same corridor.
  - Blocker: threshold support is still too qualitative. The official page only says there may be small sections of riffles when the water is low and does not tie the route to a numeric paddling floor or range.
  - Retry only if: MN DNR, Mississippi Headwaters Board materials, USGS-adjacent manager guidance, or strong same-route evidence ties the Grand Rapids gauge to a defensible numeric model for this exact route.

- 2026-04-27: `Mississippi River - Beaver Island Trail Access to Clearwater` (`threshold_weak`)
  - Fresh central-Mississippi discovery from a new same-river evidence path.
  - Official support is real: MN DNR explicitly recommends this exact 12.1-mile day trip and clearly names `Beaver Island Trail Access` and `Clearwater` as the endpoints.
  - Live-gauge support is directionally plausible: the cleanest current live story is the broader St. Cloud Mississippi gauge family.
  - Blocker: threshold support is still too qualitative. The official page only warns about riffles when river levels are low and fast-moving water when they are high, without tying the route to a numeric paddling floor or range.
  - Retry only if: MN DNR, USGS-adjacent manager guidance, outfitters, or strong same-route evidence ties a live gauge and conservative numeric threshold directly to the Beaver-Island-to-Clearwater corridor.

  - Revisit later only if: MN DNR, Mississippi Headwaters Board, or USGS-adjacent guidance publishes a route-level gauge model for the headwaters corridor.

## Rules

- Do not retry a route listed here unless new evidence directly addresses the blocker.
- Prefer genuinely new candidates over rechecking old rejected ones.
- If a route is added to PaddleTodayV2, move it to `Added`.
- If a route remains blocked, keep the exact blocker explicit and actionable.
- Treat the live V2 inventory in `src/data/rivers.ts` as the route source of truth if this file lags behind added routes.

- 2026-05-26: `Eleven Point River - Whitten Access to Riverton East River Access`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, the first Missouri V2 route.
  - Qualification path: Forest Service explicitly describes Whitten-to-Riverton as a popular 8-mile day float, publishes open public access pages and coordinates for both Whitten River Access and Riverton East River Access, and documents route-mile features including Conner Chute, Boze Mill Spring, Halls Bay, and Riverton Access. USGS `07071500` (`Eleven Point River near Bardley, MO`) is a direct lower-Eleven-Point live gauge, and American Whitewater ties the broader Highway 19-to-Highway 160 reach to the Bardley gauge.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 300 cfs, sourced from Rivers.MOHERP's Bardley gauge rating and exact Whitten-to-Riverton trip evidence. No ideal range or high-water cutoff was claimed because the numeric support is community-derived rather than an official paddling band.
  - Remaining caveats: cold spring-fed water, rapid rises after rain, possible fresh wood, Halls Bay channel choice, motorized users under the 25 hp allowance, no glass/polystyrene, no overnight camping in day-use access areas, and private land away from public access/designated/legal stops.
  - Fresh gauge clusters also reviewed and parked:
    - `St. Francis River - Millstream Gardens to Silver Mines` -> `no_live_gauge`: route/access/threshold support is strong, but the established Roselle gauge story does not currently expose a product-fetchable standard USGS instantaneous-values path for PaddleTodayV2.
    - `Flat Creek - McDowell Low Water Bridge to Stubblefield Access` -> `research_later`: USGS `07052820` and Stubblefield Access are strong, but this pass did not find enough official public put-in authority for McDowell Low Water Bridge.

- 2026-05-26: `Split Rock Creek - Split Rock Park to Palisades State Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `split-rock-creek-split-rock-park-palisades`, the first South Dakota V2 route.
  - Reconciliation result: no existing South Dakota route was present in `src/data/rivers.ts`, and no obvious South Dakota slug already covered Split Rock Creek, so this was not a duplicate add.
  - Qualification path: Sioux Empire Paddlers documents the exact 2.8-mile Split Rock Park-to-Palisades State Park route, ties it to direct `USGS 06482610` (`Split Rock Creek at Corson, SD`), and gives a numeric 4.5 ft low-water floor. Visit Garretson confirms Split Rock Park has a canoe/kayak launch downstream from the dam, and South Dakota GFP confirms canoeing/kayaking on Split Rock Creek at Palisades State Park.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 4.5 ft on the Corson gauge. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the level source is local-community guidance rather than an official manager-published paddling band.
  - Remaining caveats: this is a whitewater/moving-water creek route with Class I-II ledges, some Class III behavior near high flows around 1,000 cfs, possible fences across the creek, strainers, fast rain rises, state-park entry requirements, and mandatory same-day scouting.
  - Fresh gauge clusters also reviewed and parked:
    - `Big Sioux River - Jay Heath Canoe and Kayak Trail` -> `threshold_weak`: South Dakota GFP official trail/access mapping is real, but this pass did not find a manager-published route-specific numeric threshold package for an exact day-trip segment tied to `USGS 06482000` or another product-ready gauge.
    - `Missouri National Recreational River day-trip segments` -> `research_later`: NPS corridor support and SDCKA mapping context are real, but the current evidence is still too broad for a conservative day route because big-water wind, dam-release exposure, and lack of route-specific numeric paddling bands remain unresolved.

- 2026-05-26: `North Fork of the White River - North Fork Recreation Area / Hammond Camp to Blair Bridge Access`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`.
  - Reconciliation result: `Eleven Point River - Whitten Access to Riverton East River Access` was already the only live Missouri route and was not duplicated.
  - Qualification path: the Forest Service lists North Fork Recreation Area as open, publishes coordinates, boat-access details, and an explicit route note that Blair Bridge, operated by MDC, is the downstream take-out about 10 river miles away; MDC confirms Blair Bridge Access as public fishing/floating access with a concrete boat ramp; USGS `07057500` (`North Fork River near Tecumseh, MO`) is a standard product-fetchable same-river live gauge downstream; and Rivers.MOHERP provides Tecumseh gauge ratings plus exact Hammond Camp-to-Blair Bridge trip evidence.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 300 cfs on the Tecumseh gauge. Rivers.MOHERP's gauge page rates the gauge good at lower values, but V2 uses a higher floor because the exact Hammond-to-Blair trip evidence was logged good at 368 cfs and the gauge is downstream rather than in the reach.
  - Remaining caveats: downstream proxy-gauge fit, no official paddling band or high-water cutoff, cold spring-fed water, The Falls about 7 miles below the put-in, fresh wood/strainers after rain, ramp-to-steps carry at North Fork Recreation Area, and busy summer conditions at Blair Bridge.
  - Route-gallery result: no rights-clean exact-route asset was selected.
  - Validation: `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed; `npm.cmd test` and `npm.cmd run build` were blocked after typecheck by sandbox access-denied errors while Vite/Vitest loaded dependencies; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only.

- 2026-05-26: `Meramec River - Sappington Bridge Access to Meramec State Park`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park`.

  - Qualification path: MDC confirms Sappington Bridge Access as public Meramec boat/fishing access, the MDC St. Louis Region southern small river accesses plan explicitly says Sappington Bridge Access to Meramec State Park in Sullivan is a 5-mile canoe float, Missouri State Parks confirms the Meramec State Park concrete boat launch and canoe launch near River Stop Store, USGS `07014500` (`Meramec River near Sullivan, MO`) is the direct live gauge in the route corridor, and Rivers.MOHERP provides Sullivan-gauge condition ratings plus nearby trip logs.
  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 200 cfs on the Sullivan gauge. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the numeric support is community-calibrated rather than an official paddling band; route copy preserves MoHERP's high/flood cautions for casual paddlers.
  - Remaining caveats: no official manager-published threshold band, high/flood water, shallow riffles near the low floor, weekend rental/tubing congestion, possible fresh wood after rain, private-bank rules away from legal stops, and coordinate precision at the state park launch.
  - Route-gallery result: no rights-clean exact-route asset was selected.
  - Validation: `npm.cmd run typecheck` passed; `npm.cmd test` and `npm.cmd run build` both reran typecheck successfully but failed afterward with sandbox access-denied / dependency-resolution errors while Vite/Vitest loaded config/dependencies; `git diff --check` passed with line-ending warnings only.

- 2026-05-26: `Bryant Creek - Sycamore Access to Warren Bridge Access`
  - Added to PaddleTodayV2 as `bryant-creek-sycamore-warren-bridge`.
  - Reconciliation result: the prior `needs_manual_coordinates` Bryant Creek ledger item was resolved and marked `added`; the existing Missouri routes `eleven-point-river-whitten-riverton`, `north-fork-white-river-north-fork-blair`, and `meramec-river-sappington-bridge-meramec-state-park` were not duplicated.

  - Threshold model: conservative `minimum-only` at 300 cfs on the Tecumseh gauge. No ideal range or upper cutoff was claimed because the level source is community-calibrated rather than official manager-published paddling bands.

  - Remaining caveats: no official threshold band, high-water/rapid-rise risk, shallow shoals near the 300 cfs floor, older post-2017 flood reports of rough or formerly closed access conditions at Warren Bridge, loose gravel/deep sand at Sycamore, private land away from public/legal stops, and no rights-clean exact-route image selected.
  - Route-gallery result: no image was added because MDC access photos are not clearly reusable as local gallery assets.

## 2026-07-02 consolidation note

- Redundant unpushed route permutations from the July 2 route-add wave were consolidated into canonical multi-access route records. If an earlier note in this file now repeats a canonical slug, treat that as evidence that the former point-to-point variant was folded into the live access-planner route rather than kept as a separate route.

## 2026-07-03 17:54 America/Chicago / 2026-07-03 22:54 UTC Kentucky implementation pass

- Treated the live repo state at run start as the baseline and expanded the current tree from `367` to `370` route slugs by adding `upper-cumberland-river-4-mile-artemus`, `upper-cumberland-river-varilla-artemus`, and `upper-cumberland-river-4-mile-barbourville`.
- Qualification path: current KDFWR Upper Cumberland River guidance still documents the exact public site-to-site mileages of 4 Mile -> Artemus `9.8` and Artemus -> Barbourville `5.1`, which also support the new Varilla -> Artemus combination by adding the existing official Varilla -> 4 Mile `13.8` segment for about `23.6` miles total; the same page still publishes the official Williamsburg ladder of Low below `300 cfs / 3.0 ft`, Good `300-700 cfs / 3.0-3.8 ft`, and High above those marks; current KDFWR access-detail pages still expose source-backed coordinates and public-use details for Varilla Ramp, 4 Mile Ramp, Artemus Rock Access, and Barbourville Ramp; and same-day direct USGS Water Services returned `1010 cfs / 4.20 ft` at `2026-07-03 18:00 EDT` for direct gauge `03404000`.
- Added camping and safety/logistics notes for all three routes. Camping stays `none` for the Artemus finishes and `endpoint_campground` for the Barbourville finish because Thompson Park still includes RV camping support.
- No gallery image was added; bounded KDFWR, Commons, and same-river USGS media review did not produce a clearly rights-clean exact-route asset for any of the three new slugs, and `docs/river-image-source-audit.csv` records the no-image outcomes.

## 2026-07-11 22:45 America/Chicago / 2026-07-12 03:45 UTC Goose Creek split restoration pass

- Treated the live repo state at run start as the baseline and expanded the current tree from `484` to `487` route slugs by restoring `goose-creek-jacks-tobacco-road`, `goose-creek-tobacco-road-dump-hollow`, and `goose-creek-dump-hollow-laurel-branch`.
- Qualification path: current KDFWR Goose Creek guidance still documents Jacks / Bowling Branch Bridge Access -> Tobacco Road Bridge Ramp `2.2` miles, Tobacco Road Bridge Ramp -> Dump Hollow Ford `0.8` mile, and Dump Hollow Ford -> Laurel Branch Road Access `4.8` miles; the same page still publishes the official Manchester ladder of Low below `175 cfs / 7.4 ft`, Good `175-514 cfs / 7.4-8.5 ft`, and High above those marks; current KDFWR access-detail pages still expose source-backed coordinates, year-round availability, and no-camping roadside access details for Jacks, Tobacco Road, Dump Hollow, and Laurel Branch; and same-day direct USGS Water Services returned `19.9 cfs / 6.02 ft` at `2026-07-11 22:45 EDT` for direct gauge `03281100`.
- Added camping and safety/logistics notes for all three routes. Camping stays `none` because all four Goose Creek accesses are simple roadside carry-down or ford-style sites with no documented overnight support.
- No gallery image was added; bounded Commons, KDFWR access imagery, and same-river USGS/general web review did not produce a clearly rights-clean exact-route asset for any of the three restored slugs, and `docs/river-image-source-audit.csv` records the no-image outcomes.
